name: Moralta
description: Moralta is a legendary warhorn, said to have been forged by dwarves under the patronage of a benevolent forest spirit during the reign of Charlemagne. It is described as being crafted from the horn of a mythical Aurochs, reinforced with bands of silver and etched with runes that glow faintly in the presence of injustice. Moralta's primary power lies in its ability to inspire courage and unwavering loyalty in those who hear its call, turning even the most timid soldiers into fearless warriors. The horn's sound is also said to possess the power to shatter illusions and reveal the truth, making it invaluable in discerning treachery and deceit. Furthermore, it is rumored that Moralta can summon the spirits of fallen heroes to aid in battle, providing invaluable tactical guidance and bolstering morale. The horn was a symbol of Charlemagne's divinely ordained right to rule and a testament to the strength and righteousness of the Frankish kingdom. It was believed to be kept in the treasury of Aachen, only to be used in times of dire need. Legends suggest that the horn would only sound for a truly worthy leader, one who embodies the virtues of justice, compassion, and strength. The loss of Moralta is often seen as a precursor to the decline of the Carolingian dynasty, symbolizing a loss of moral compass and the fragmentation of the empire.
subtitle: The Horn of Courage and Truth

keyMyths.title: The Forging of Moralta
keyMyths.description: Dwarves, blessed by a forest spirit, create Moralta for Charlemagne, imbuing it with powers of courage and truth. The horn is a symbol of his righteous rule.
keyMyths.source: Legendary Tales of the Carolingian Court

keyMyths.title: Moralta at Roncevaux Pass
keyMyths.description: Some versions claim Roland attempted to blow Moralta to call for aid at Roncevaux, but it was too late. This is debated, as Olifant is the more commonly known horn in that context.

sources.author: Einhard
sources.description: Biography of Charlemagne, provides context for Carolingian ideals.
